title: Acute Liver Failure
clinicalContext: A 32-year-old woman presents to the ED with confusion, jaundice, and RUQ tenderness. She reports taking 'extra' acetaminophen for back pain over the past 5 days. She appears disoriented, has asterixis, and labs reveal AST 8,500 U/L, ALT 7,200 U/L, INR 4.2, total bilirubin 6.8 mg/dL, creatinine 2.1 mg/dL, and ammonia 142 µmol/L.
learningObjectives: Define acute liver failure and distinguish it from acute-on-chronic liver failure
Understand the pathophysiology of hepatic encephalopathy and cerebral edema in ALF
Identify common etiologies with focus on acetaminophen toxicity
Apply the Rumack-Matthew nomogram and understand N-acetylcysteine therapy
Use King's College Criteria for transplant decision-making
Recognize and manage complications including coagulopathy, cerebral edema, and multiorgan failure

layers.name: Definition & Classification
layers.description: Defining ALF and distinguishing it from other liver syndromes

questions.question: What is the definition of acute liver failure (ALF)?
questions.answer: ALF is defined as severe acute liver injury with encephalopathy and coagulopathy (INR ≥1.5) in a patient WITHOUT pre-existing chronic liver disease, occurring within 26 weeks of symptom onset. The presence of encephalopathy distinguishes ALF from acute liver injury.

whyChain.why: Why is the absence of chronic liver disease important?
whyChain.answer: In patients with chronic liver disease who develop acute decompensation, the syndrome is called 'acute-on-chronic liver failure' (ACLF), which has different pathophysiology, management, and prognosis than true ALF.

questions.question: How is ALF classified by the O'Grady system, and why does timing matter?
questions.answer: The O'Grady classification divides ALF by time from jaundice to encephalopathy: Hyperacute (<7 days), Acute (7-21 days), and Subacute (>21 days to 26 weeks). Hyperacute ALF (e.g., acetaminophen) has higher cerebral edema risk but BETTER transplant-free survival. Subacute has less edema but WORSE prognosis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why does hyperacute liver failure have better prognosis despite more cerebral edema?
whyChain.answer: Hyperacute presentations (like acetaminophen) cause massive hepatocyte necrosis that, if survived, allows rapid hepatocyte regeneration. Subacute presentations often reflect ongoing immune injury or viral hepatitis where regeneration is impaired.

questions.question: What are the major causes of acute liver failure in the United States?
questions.answer: In the US, the leading causes are: (1) Acetaminophen toxicity (~46%), (2) Indeterminate (~14%), (3) Drug-induced liver injury - idiosyncratic (~11%), (4) Hepatitis B (~7%), (5) Autoimmune hepatitis (~6%), (6) Ischemic hepatitis (~5%). Viral hepatitis A and E are more common in developing countries.

sources.text: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

whyChain.why: Why is acetaminophen toxicity so common?
whyChain.answer: Acetaminophen is available over-the-counter, present in numerous combination products, and has a narrow therapeutic window. Many cases are unintentional from therapeutic misadventure (taking multiple products containing APAP) rather than suicidal intent.
whyChain.source: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

questions.question: What laboratory pattern suggests acetaminophen toxicity vs. other causes?
questions.answer: Acetaminophen toxicity typically shows: Very high AST/ALT (often >3,000-10,000 U/L), relatively LOW bilirubin initially (AST:bilirubin ratio >500:1), and AST often > ALT. This pattern also occurs in ischemic hepatitis. Viral hepatitis and autoimmune causes tend to have lower transaminases with higher bilirubin.

whyChain.why: Why is bilirubin relatively low in acetaminophen toxicity?
whyChain.answer: Bilirubin elevation requires time for conjugation and excretion dysfunction to manifest. In hyperacute presentations like APAP toxicity, massive hepatocyte death occurs before significant bilirubin accumulation. Rising bilirubin over subsequent days indicates poor prognosis.

questions.question: What initial workup should be ordered for all patients with suspected ALF?
questions.answer: Essential workup includes: Acetaminophen level, viral hepatitis panel (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HBc IgM, HCV Ab, HEV IgM), autoimmune markers (ANA, ASMA, IgG levels), toxicology screen, ceruloplasmin/copper (Wilson's), pregnancy test, HSV PCR, and imaging (ultrasound with Doppler to exclude Budd-Chiari).

whyChain.why: Why is HSV PCR important in the workup?
whyChain.answer: HSV hepatitis has >80% mortality if untreated but responds to acyclovir. It can occur without mucocutaneous lesions. Empiric IV acyclovir should be considered in all ALF patients pending viral workup.

layers.name: Pathophysiology
layers.description: Understanding the mechanisms of liver failure and its complications

questions.question: What is the mechanism of acetaminophen-induced hepatotoxicity?
questions.answer: At therapeutic doses, acetaminophen is metabolized by glucuronidation and sulfation. When these pathways are saturated, CYP2E1 converts APAP to the toxic metabolite NAPQI. Normally, NAPQI is detoxified by glutathione. In overdose, glutathione is depleted, and NAPQI accumulates causing centrilobular (Zone 3) hepatocyte necrosis.

sources.text: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

whyChain.why: Why does Zone 3 (centrilobular) injury predominate?
whyChain.answer: Zone 3 hepatocytes have the highest concentration of CYP2E1 (the enzyme that produces NAPQI) and the lowest oxygen tension. This combination makes them most vulnerable to NAPQI-mediated oxidative damage.

whyChain.why: Why does N-acetylcysteine work as an antidote?
whyChain.answer: NAC is a glutathione precursor that replenishes hepatic glutathione stores, allowing continued detoxification of NAPQI. It also has direct antioxidant effects and improves microcirculatory blood flow and oxygen delivery.
whyChain.source: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of hepatic encephalopathy and cerebral edema in ALF?
questions.answer: In ALF, the failing liver cannot clear ammonia, which crosses the blood-brain barrier. Astrocytes convert ammonia + glutamate to glutamine (via glutamine synthetase). Glutamine acts as an osmolyte, causing astrocyte swelling. Unlike chronic liver disease, the brain hasn't adapted, so cytotoxic edema and intracranial hypertension develop rapidly.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why don't patients with chronic liver disease develop cerebral edema like ALF patients?
whyChain.answer: In chronic liver disease, the brain has time to develop compensatory mechanisms including export of organic osmolytes (myo-inositol, taurine) to balance the increased glutamine. In ALF, this adaptation cannot occur rapidly enough.

whyChain.why: At what ammonia level does intracranial hypertension become a concern?
whyChain.answer: Arterial ammonia >150 µmol/L is associated with increased risk of intracranial hypertension and cerebral herniation. However, multiorgan failure and infection can contribute to cerebral edema even at lower ammonia levels.
whyChain.source: Cerebral Oedema in ALF. World J Gastroenterol. 2013

questions.question: What are the grades of hepatic encephalopathy in ALF?
questions.answer: Grade I: Subtle personality changes, sleep disturbance, impaired attention. Grade II: Lethargy, confusion, disorientation, asterixis present. Grade III: Somnolent but arousable, marked confusion, incoherent speech. Grade IV: Coma, unresponsive to verbal/painful stimuli. Grades III-IV require intubation for airway protection.

whyChain.why: Why is grade of encephalopathy prognostically important?
whyChain.answer: Higher grades of encephalopathy correlate with increased cerebral edema risk and worse outcomes. Patients who progress to grade III-IV encephalopathy have significantly higher mortality and are more likely to require transplantation.

questions.question: What clinical signs suggest elevated intracranial pressure in ALF?
questions.answer: Signs of elevated ICP include: Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations), pupil abnormalities (sluggish or fixed dilated pupils), decerebrate or decorticate posturing, and loss of brainstem reflexes. However, these are LATE signs - by the time they appear, herniation may be imminent.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: How can ICP be monitored in ALF patients?
whyChain.answer: ICP monitoring with an intracranial bolt or catheter can be considered in grade III-IV encephalopathy, though this is controversial due to coagulopathy-related bleeding risk. Non-invasive methods include transcranial Doppler and optic nerve sheath diameter measurement.

questions.question: How is the Rumack-Matthew nomogram used, and what are its limitations?
questions.answer: The nomogram plots serum APAP level against time since ingestion to predict hepatotoxicity risk. A level above the treatment line (starting at 150 µg/mL at 4 hours) indicates NAC treatment. Limitations: Only valid for single acute ingestions with known time; NOT valid for chronic/staggered ingestions or unknown timing.

sources.text: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

whyChain.why: What if the time of ingestion is unknown?
whyChain.answer: If ingestion time is unknown or if the patient has a repeated supratherapeutic ingestion (RSTI), the nomogram cannot be used. In these cases, treat with NAC if: APAP level is detectable, transaminases are elevated, or there's a plausible history of significant APAP ingestion.

questions.question: What is the IV N-acetylcysteine protocol for acetaminophen toxicity?
questions.answer: The standard 21-hour IV NAC protocol: Loading dose 150 mg/kg over 1 hour, then 50 mg/kg over 4 hours, then 100 mg/kg over 16 hours. Continue NAC if: ALT remains elevated, INR >2, APAP still detectable, or encephalopathy present. NAC is most effective within 8 hours but beneficial even at 24+ hours.

sources.text: AASLD Practice Guidance on DILI. Hepatology. 2023

whyChain.why: Should NAC be used in non-acetaminophen ALF?
whyChain.answer: Yes. A prospective trial showed NAC improved transplant-free survival in non-APAP ALF, particularly in early-stage encephalopathy (grades I-II). Proposed mechanisms include improved oxygen delivery and microcirculation. AASLD recommends empiric NAC for all ALF.

layers.name: Prognosis & Transplant Criteria
layers.description: King's College Criteria and transplant decision-making

questions.question: What are the King's College Criteria for liver transplantation in acetaminophen-induced ALF?
questions.answer: For APAP-induced ALF, transplant is indicated if: Arterial pH <7.30 after resuscitation, OR all three of: (1) Grade III-IV encephalopathy, (2) INR >6.5 (PT >100 sec), AND (3) Creatinine >3.4 mg/dL. These criteria have ~90% specificity for death without transplant.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: What is the sensitivity of King's College Criteria?
whyChain.answer: Sensitivity is approximately 68%, meaning ~30% of patients who will die without transplant may NOT meet criteria. Serial assessment and clinical judgment remain essential. Additional markers like lactate >3.5 mmol/L or phosphate >3.75 mg/dL may identify high-risk patients earlier.
whyChain.source: King's College Criteria Meta-Analysis. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2016

questions.question: What are the King's College Criteria for NON-acetaminophen ALF?
questions.answer: For non-APAP ALF, transplant is indicated if: INR >6.5 (regardless of encephalopathy grade), OR any 3 of: (1) Age <10 or >40 years, (2) Etiology: non-A/non-B hepatitis, drug-induced, Wilson's, (3) Jaundice >7 days before encephalopathy, (4) INR >3.5, (5) Bilirubin >17.5 mg/dL.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why do different criteria apply for APAP vs non-APAP?
whyChain.answer: APAP causes hyperacute liver failure with high regeneration potential if survived. Non-APAP causes (viral, autoimmune, idiosyncratic DILI) often have slower onset and impaired regeneration. Different prognostic factors predict outcomes in each group.
whyChain.source: King's College Criteria Meta-Analysis. Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2016

questions.question: What is the transplant-free survival rate in ALF by etiology?
questions.answer: Transplant-free survival varies by etiology: Acetaminophen ~70%, Hepatitis A ~70%, Ischemic hepatitis ~55%, Pregnancy-related (AFLP, HELLP) ~60%, Autoimmune ~45%, Hepatitis B ~30%, Indeterminate ~25%, Drug-induced (idiosyncratic) ~25%. Overall ALF survival has improved to ~65% with modern intensive care.

whyChain.why: Why is indeterminate etiology associated with poor prognosis?
whyChain.answer: Indeterminate ALF likely represents a heterogeneous group including unrecognized APAP toxicity, drug reactions, and autoimmune hepatitis. Without a specific treatable cause identified, targeted therapy is not possible, and these patients tend to have subacute presentations with poor regeneration.

questions.question: How should coagulopathy be managed in ALF?
questions.answer: Do NOT routinely correct INR with FFP unless active bleeding or before invasive procedures. The INR is a prognostic marker - correcting it obscures assessment of hepatic function and transplant criteria. Vitamin K can be given empirically. Paradoxically, ALF patients have a 'rebalanced' hemostasis and are at risk for both bleeding AND thrombosis.

whyChain.why: Why is there a 'rebalanced' hemostasis despite elevated INR?
whyChain.answer: The liver synthesizes both procoagulant factors (measured by INR) AND anticoagulant factors (protein C, protein S, antithrombin). In ALF, both are reduced proportionally, so the net hemostatic balance may be preserved despite abnormal laboratory values.

questions.question: How should cerebral edema be prevented and treated in ALF?
questions.answer: Prevention: Head of bed 30°, avoid hyperthermia, maintain normal sodium (or mild hypernatremia 145-150), avoid hypotension, minimize stimulation. Treatment of elevated ICP: Mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg (if serum osm <320), hypertonic saline (target Na 145-155), consider hypothermia (32-35°C), barbiturate coma in refractory cases.

sources.text: StatPearls: Acute Liver Failure. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

whyChain.why: Why avoid hyperthermia in ALF?
whyChain.answer: Fever increases cerebral metabolic demand and ammonia production, worsening cerebral edema. Mild hypothermia has been shown to reduce brain ammonia, glutamate, and lactate, though its effect on survival is unproven.

questions.question: What is the role of renal replacement therapy in ALF?
questions.answer: Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) is preferred over intermittent hemodialysis. Benefits include: ammonia clearance, correction of metabolic derangements, fluid management, and potentially reduced cerebral edema. CRRT may be initiated even without traditional AKI indications to help control ammonia levels.

whyChain.why: Why is continuous RRT preferred over intermittent?
whyChain.answer: Intermittent hemodialysis causes rapid fluid and electrolyte shifts that can increase cerebral edema and hemodynamic instability. CRRT provides gradual, continuous correction with better hemodynamic tolerance.

questions.question: When should lactulose be used in ALF, and are there concerns?
questions.answer: Lactulose may be used in early encephalopathy (grades I-II) to reduce gut ammonia production. However, avoid excessive diarrhea - it can cause dehydration, electrolyte disturbances, and bowel distension that complicates transplant surgery. Lactulose is less critical in ALF than in chronic liver disease because hyperacute ammonia accumulation overwhelms gut-based interventions.

whyChain.why: Why is lactulose less effective in ALF than chronic liver failure?
whyChain.answer: In ALF, ammonia production exceeds the gut's contribution. The massive hepatocyte necrosis releases intracellular contents, and impaired urea cycle function allows systemic ammonia accumulation that cannot be meaningfully reduced by altering gut flora alone.

questions.question: What are the key time-sensitive decisions in ALF management?
questions.answer: Critical time-sensitive decisions: (1) Start NAC within 8 hours of APAP ingestion if possible (beneficial even later), (2) Transfer to transplant center BEFORE meeting KCC (logistics take time), (3) Intubate for grade III-IV encephalopathy before airway compromise, (4) List for transplant when criteria met (organ availability varies), (5) Manage infections aggressively (>80% of ALF patients develop infection).

whyChain.why: Why transfer to a transplant center before meeting KCC?
whyChain.answer: ALF can deteriorate rapidly. Transfer, transplant evaluation, and organ procurement take significant time. Waiting until criteria are met may result in the patient becoming too sick for transplant (cerebral herniation, uncontrolled sepsis) or dying before an organ is available.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of acute liver failure. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing acute liver failure, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
